title: The Infinite Glossary of Technical Architecture

slides.title: PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor)
slides.content: PHP is a venerable yet powerful server-side scripting language that has powered the web for decades. In the FRAISE architecture, PHP serves as the bedrock of our legacy CMS core found in `repo-php/`. It excels at dynamic content generation, session management, and database orchestration. Modern PHP (8.x+) has undergone a massive renaissance, introducing Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation, union types, and significant performance optimizations that rival compiled languages for web workloads. Within our multi-tenant system, PHP interprets incoming HTTP requests, bootstraps the application environment, and renders complex templates using a custom hierarchy engine. Its share-nothing architecture ensures that each request is isolated, providing inherent security and stability. As you explore the `repo-php/` directory, you will see how PHP bridges the gap between raw data and polished HTML interfaces, serving as the 'glue' that coordinates plugins, themes, and core system utilities in a seamless request-response lifecycle.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><path d='M120 60 L280 60 L280 140 L120 140 Z' fill='#3b82f6' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='30' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.2'/><path d='M180 100 H220 M200 80 V120' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#1e40af' font-weight='600'>Server-Side Logic Engine</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the lifecycle of a PHP request from the server receiving an HTTP hit to the browser receiving HTML.
Compare PHP's 'Share-Nothing' architecture with Node.js's event-loop model for high-concurrency tasks.
How do modern PHP features like Attributes and JIT compilation change the way we design large-scale applications?

slides.title: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
slides.content: The Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern is the gold standard for organizing large-scale applications. In our ecosystem, this separation of concerns is implemented with surgical precision. The 'Model' (`repo-php/class/Model.php`) encapsulates the data logic and database schemas, ensuring that raw SQL never leaks into the presentation layer. The 'View' (`repo-php/views/`) handles the final output, utilizing modular PHP templates to produce SEO-friendly HTML. The 'Controller' (`repo-php/class/Controller.php`) acts as the conductor, receiving input from the router, querying the models, and passing prepared data to the views. This architecture allows multiple developers to work on different aspects of the same feature without stepping on each other's toes—a designer can refine a View while a backend engineer optimizes a Model. By strictly adhering to MVC, we ensure that our CMS remains maintainable, testable, and scalable as we add more complex features like AI-driven content generation or real-time analytics dashboards.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><rect x='80' y='120' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#22c55e' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#22c55e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='240' y='120' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#22c55e' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#22c55e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='160' y='40' width='80' height='40' rx='4' fill='#16a34a' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M200 80 V100 M120 120 L180 80 M220 80 L280 120' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='120' y='150' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#14532d'>Model</text><text x='200' y='70' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#14532d'>Controller</text><text x='280' y='150' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#14532d'>View</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Identify a file in repo-php and explain which part of the MVC pattern it represents and why.
How does the MVC pattern prevent 'Spaghetti Code' in a multi-tenant CMS environment?
Discuss a scenario where strictly following MVC might lead to over-engineering. How can we find a balance?

slides.title: CMS (Content Management System)
slides.content: A Content Management System (CMS) is the interface between technical infrastructure and human creativity. Paradoxically, the best CMS is one that disappears, allowing users to focus entirely on their narrative without worrying about server configurations or HTML tags. Our implementation is a 'Multi-Tenant' behemoth, capable of serving thousands of unique sites from a single atomic codebase. The `CMS` orchestrator in `repo-php/class/CMS.php` is responsible for 'Site Resolution'—detecting the hostname, loading the correct configuration from the `content/` directory, and initializing the specific plugins required for that instance. This 'headless-ready' architecture means we can serve content via traditional PHP templates OR expose it as a JSON API for a React/Vue frontend. By mastering this system, you learn not just how to manage text, but how to build a flexible platform that can evolve from a simple blog engine into a sophisticated enterprise data hub, all while maintaining strict security and performance boundaries.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fff1f2'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='200' height='80' rx='8' fill='#e11d48' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#e11d48' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M130 90 H270 M130 110 H220' stroke='#e11d48' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M250 110 L270 130 M270 110 L250 130' stroke='#e11d48' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#881337' font-weight='600'>Content Orchestration Layer</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What are the security risks of a multi-tenant CMS compared to a single-site installation?
Explain the process of 'Site Resolution' in the FRAISE CMS core.
How would you modify the CMS class to support 'draft' vs 'published' content states globally?

slides.title: PSR (PHP Standard Recommendation)
slides.content: Standardization is what transforms a collection of scripts into a professional software engineering ecosystem. The PHP Standard Recommendations (PSR), published by the PHP Framework Interop Group (FIG), provide a common language and set of rules for PHP developers worldwide. Our codebase strictly adheres to PSR-12 (Coding Style) and PSR-4 (Autoloading). This means that every class name, indentation level, and file structure is predictable. By following PSR-4, we eliminate the need for manual `include` statements; the autoloader simply maps namespace `CMS\Core` to directory `repo-php/class/` automatically. This predictability is critical for AI-assisted development—because the code follows a standardized 'shape,' the Gemini model can reason about the codebase with 100% accuracy. Mastering PSR isn't just about 'cleaning up' code; it's about building interoperable software that can seamlessly integrate with the millions of packages available in the global PHP landscape.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf4ff'/><rect x='150' y='50' width='100' height='100' rx='10' fill='#a21caf' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M170 80 L190 100 L230 70' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='3' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M170 120 H230' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#701a75' font-weight='600'>Predictable Standardized Code</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is PSR-4 considered the most important standard in the modern PHP ecosystem?
How does following a standard like PSR-12 improve the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) when generating code?
Find a section of repo-php that doesn't follow PSR-12 and suggest a refactoring plan.

slides.title: Composer
slides.content: In the modern era of web development, we 'stand on the shoulders of giants.' Composer is the tool that makes this possible for PHP. As a dependency manager, it allows us to import battle-tested libraries for routing, image processing, AI integration, and security without reinventing the wheel. The `composer.json` file is a manifest of our project's DNA—it lists exactly which versions of which libraries are required to run the system. When you run `composer install`, it builds a `vendor/` directory containing millions of lines of optimized code that our app can use instantly. Beyond just downloading files, Composer handles autoloading orchestration and version conflict resolution, ensuring that our polyglot stack remains stable. In the FRAISE environment, we use Composer within our Docker containers to ensure that every environment—development, staging, and production—utilizes perfectly synced library versions, eliminating the risk of 'dependency hell.'
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f5f3ff'/><path d='M150 50 L250 50 L280 150 L120 150 Z' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M180 80 Q200 100 220 80' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><circle cx='200' cy='120' r='15' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#5b21b6' font-weight='600'>Dependency Symphony</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the difference between `composer.json` and `composer.lock` and why you should NEVER manually edit the lock file.
How does Composer's autoloading mechanism work in conjunction with PSR-4 standards?
Search Packagist for a library that could improve the CMS's image handling and explain how you would integrate it using Composer.

slides.title: Nginx
slides.content: In the world of high-performance web architecture, Nginx is unsurpassed as a reverse proxy and load balancer. Within our stack, Nginx acts as the 'Security Guard' and 'Receptionist' at the main gateway. Unlike old-school servers that spawn a new process for every connection, Nginx's asynchronous, event-driven architecture allows it to handle tens of thousands of concurrent connections with minimal memory overhead. It serves static assets like CSS and images at lightning speed while intelligently proxying dynamic requests to our PHP-FPM or Node.js backends. Our `nginx.conf` is tuned for maximum efficiency, handling redirect logic, SSL termination, and rate limiting. By mastering Nginx, you gain control over the 'Edge' of your application, ensuring that your CMS is not just functional, but resilient against high-traffic surges and resource-exhaustion attacks.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0f9ff'/><path d='M100 100 L150 100 M250 100 L300 100' stroke='#0369a1' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='100' height='80' rx='8' fill='#0ea5e9' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0ea5e9' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M165 80 L235 120 M165 120 L235 80' stroke='#0ea5e9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#075985' font-weight='600'>High-Performance Reverse Proxy</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is the difference between a reverse proxy and a forward proxy in a modern web stack?
How does Nginx's 'Event-Driven' architecture differ from a traditional thread-per-connection model?
Examine the nginx.conf in repo-php and explain how it handles traffic for different file types.

slides.title: Docker & Docker Compose
slides.content: Containerization has solved the 'it works on my machine' problem once and for all. Docker allows us to package our polyglot application—PHP, Go, Rust, and Python—into immutable, predictable units. These containers include everything needed to run: the OS, libraries, environment variables, and the code itself. Docker Compose orchestrates these units, defining how our database, cache, and application servers communicate on a private virtual network. In our FRAISE environment, we use multi-stage builds to create lean, production-ready images while maintaining a rich development environment. This 'Infrastructure as Code' approach ensures that whether you are deploying to a local laptop or a global cloud cluster, the execution environment remains absolutely identical. This consistency is the foundation of modern DevOps, allowing for zero-downtime updates and seamless horizontal scaling.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#ecfeff'/><rect x='120' y='70' width='60' height='40' rx='4' fill='#0891b2' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0891b2' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='200' y='70' width='60' height='40' rx='4' fill='#0891b2' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0891b2' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='160' y='110' width='60' height='40' rx='4' fill='#0891b2' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0891b2' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M100 160 H300' stroke='#0891b2' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#155e75' font-weight='600'>Containerized Predictability</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does a Docker image differ from a Virtual Machine in terms of resource utilization and isolation?
Explain the benefit of using Docker Compose to orchestrate multiple microservices in a single development environment.
Why are multi-stage Docker builds critical for security and performance in production environments?

slides.title: PDO (PHP Data Objects)
slides.content: The database is the heart of any CMS, and PDO is its primary safeguard. PHP Data Objects (PDO) provides a consistent, database-agnostic interface for interacting with various storage engines like SQLite, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL. Beyond just 'making queries,' PDO enforces modern security best practices through 'Prepared Statements.' By decoupling the SQL structure from the user-provided data, PDO makes SQL injection attacks—historically the most common web vulnerability—mathematically impossible. In our `DB` utility class, we leverage PDO to handle complex relational queries, transaction management, and error reporting. This abstraction layer means we can swap our underlying database from SQLite to a high-availability MySQL cluster with minimal code changes. Mastering PDO is essential for building data-rich applications that are both fast and secure against malicious exploitation.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fffbeb'/><ellipse cx='200' cy='130' rx='80' ry='30' fill='#d97706' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#d97706' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 130 V100 Q120 70 200 70 Q280 70 280 100 V130' fill='none' stroke='#d97706' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='180' y='90' width='40' height='30' rx='4' fill='#d97706' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#d97706' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#92400e' font-weight='600'>Secure Data Abstraction</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain why prepared statements are more secure than concatenating variables directly into an SQL string.
How does PDO facilitate 'Database Portability' and why is this important for a growing product?
Discuss a scenario where you would use Database Transactions with PDO to ensure data integrity.

slides.title: Router
slides.content: A Router is the GPS of your application architecture. It is the logical component that translates a 'human-readable' URL like `/blog/mastering-ai` into an 'executable' command in your backend. Our custom Router in `repo-php/class/Router.php` analyzes the incoming Request URI and HTTP Method (GET, POST) to determine which Controller and Action should be executed. This system allows us to implement 'RESTful' API patterns and 'SEO-friendly' Permalinks without exposing the ugly internal file structure of the server. By decoupling the URL from the file path, we can refactor our backend logic without ever breaking the links shared by our users. A professional routing system is what distinguishes a collection of scripts from a unified, navigable platform, providing the structure necessary for complex interactions and deep-linked state management.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf2f8'/><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='10' fill='#be185d'/><path d='M110 100 L200 60 M110 100 L200 100 M110 100 L200 140' stroke='#be185d' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><rect x='200' y='50' width='80' height='20' rx='4' fill='#be185d' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#be185d' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='200' y='90' width='80' height='20' rx='4' fill='#be185d' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#be185d' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='200' y='130' width='80' height='20' rx='4' fill='#be185d' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#be185d' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#831843' font-weight='600'>URL Selection Logic</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Compare the 'PushState' client-side routing in Vue with the traditional server-side routing in PHP.
How can a Router improve a site's SEO ranking compared to using raw `.php` file extensions in URLs?
Design a route pattern for a user profile page that includes both a username and a specific tab (e.g., `/user/alice/photos`).

slides.title: Plugin Architecture
slides.content: In software engineering, the Open-Closed Principle states that software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. Our Plugin Architecture is the physical embodiment of this law. By using the `PluginManager`, we can add massive new capabilities—like real-time analytics, automated SEO optimization, or AI form handling—without touching a single line of the 'Core' CMS logic. Plugins live in their own self-contained directories, hooking into the CMS via a registry of events and triggers. This modularity means that bugs in a third-party plugin are isolated, protecting the stability of the entire system. Furthermore, it allows for a vibrant ecosystem where developers can share and install extensions with a simple drag-and-drop or Git pull. Mastering the plugin pattern is the key to building platforms that last for decades, as it allows the system to evolve organically alongside changing technical requirements without collapsing under the weight of 'feature creep.'
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='200' height='80' rx='8' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='280' y='80' width='30' height='40' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M300 90 V110' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='4' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M250 100 H280' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='3'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#115e59' font-weight='600'>Isolatable Extensions</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What are the primary benefits of an 'Open-Closed' architecture for an enterprise-level CMS?
Explain the process of 'Hooking' a plugin into the CMS lifecycle—how does the core know when to call the plugin?
Discuss the trade-offs between a 'Built-in Feature' and a 'Plugin' for a new major system capability.

slides.title: Vue 3 (Composition API)
slides.content: Vue 3 represents a paradigm shift in how we build interactive user interfaces. The introduction of the Composition API allows us to group logical concerns together rather than scattering them across various lifecycle hooks like `data`, `methods`, and `mounted`. This 'function-based' approach makes complex components like our `SiteEditor.vue` far more readable and maintainable. By using `setup`, we can extract related logic into 'Composables'—reusable functions that can be shared across the entire application. This modularity is a direct implementation of the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle. Vue's underlying reactivity system, now powered by JavaScript Proxies, ensures that every state change is detected with surgical precision, triggering minimal and efficient re-renders. As you build the FRAISE dashboard, you are leveraging the most modern features of the Vue ecosystem to create an experience that is as fluid as a desktop application while remaining light enough for the mobile web.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><path d='M200 40 L300 200 L100 200 Z' fill='#42b883' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#42b883' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M200 80 L260 180 L140 180 Z' fill='#35495e' fill-opacity='0.8'/><circle cx='200' cy='130' r='20' fill='#fff' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#fff' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#166534' font-weight='600'>Composition Over Options</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is the primary advantage of the Composition API over the Options API for complex components with hundreds of lines of code?
Explain how the `setup` script block simplifies component declarations.
How can you use 'Composables' to share stateful logic between the SiteExplorer and SiteEditor views?

slides.title: Vite
slides.content: Vite, which means 'Fast' in French, is the next-generation frontend tool that powers our build process. It fundamentally changes the development experience by leveraging native ES Modules (ESM) in the browser. Unlike older tools like Webpack that bundle the entire application before starting the server, Vite serves your code instantly and only bundles what is requested. This results in a development server that starts in milliseconds, regardless of the project size. When it's time for production, Vite uses Rollup to generate highly optimized, minified bundles that load lightning-fast for your end users. Its 'Hot Module Replacement' (HMR) capability ensures that when you save a file, only the affected component is updated in the browser, preserving the application state. In the FRAISE stack, Vite is the invisible orchestrator that transforms our TypeScript and Vue files into the high-performance assets that define our user experience.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf4ff'/><path d='M200 40 L240 100 L200 100 L230 160 L140 80 L180 80 Z' fill='#bd34fe' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#bd34fe' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='60' fill='none' stroke='#ffc91f' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#701a75' font-weight='600'>Instant Dev Feedback</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is Vite significantly faster than Webpack during development for large enterprise applications?
Explain the role of 'Native ES Modules' in Vite's architecture.
How does Vite optimize our production bundle differently from our development server?

slides.title: Tailwind CSS
slides.content: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework that has revolutionized how we style modern web applications. Instead of writing custom CSS rules in separate files—which often leads to 'specificity wars' and unmaintainable stylesheets—we apply low-level utility classes directly in our HTML and Vue templates. Classes like `bg-blue-500`, `p-4`, and `flex` give us total control over the design without ever leaving our component files. This approach ensures a consistent design system across the entire FRAISE platform, as we are limited to a predefined set of spacing, color, and typography scales. Because Tailwind uses a Just-In-Time (JIT) engine, it only generates the CSS that is actually used in the project, resulting in production bundles that are remarkably small. By mastering Tailwind, you can move from a mockup to a functional, responsive UI in minutes, ensuring that our CMS looks professional and polished on every device.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0f9ff'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='40' height='40' rx='4' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='40' height='40' rx='4' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='200' y='60' width='100' height='40' rx='4' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='100' y='110' width='200' height='40' rx='4' fill='#38bdf8' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#075985' font-weight='600'>Utility-First Design System</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the benefit of 'Utility-First' CSS over traditional 'Semantic' CSS in a large components-based project.
How does Tailwind's JIT engine handle custom values that aren't in the default theme (e.g., `top-[117px]`)?
Why does using Tailwind practically eliminate the risk of 'CSS Bloat' as a project grows over time?

slides.title: Props & Events
slides.content: The architecture of a modern component-based UI is built on a foundation of 'One-Way Data Flow.' Props and Events are the two pillars of this communication. 'Props' (Properties) allow data to flow down from a parent component to its children, providing the necessary 'state' for the child to render correctly. This is a read-only contract; a child should never mutate a prop. When a child needs to communicate back to the parent—for example, when a user clicks a 'Delete' button in a card—it 'Emits' an Event. The parent hears this event and decides how to update the global state. This strict pattern makes debugging incredibly easy, as you can clearly trace where data originates and where changes are triggered. In the FRAISE dashboard, this allows us to have a complex view like `SiteExplorer.vue` that manages dozens of individual `SiteCard` components with perfect synchronization and no 'spaghetti state' entanglement.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fff7ed'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='100' height='40' rx='4' fill='#ea580c' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='120' width='100' height='40' rx='4' fill='#ea580c' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M180 80 V120' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M175 110 L180 120 L185 110' fill='none' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M220 120 V80' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M215 90 L220 80 L225 90' fill='none' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2'/><text x='170' y='105' text-anchor='end' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#9a3412'>Props</text><text x='230' y='105' text-anchor='start' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#9a3412'>Events</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is it considered a 'Code Smell' to mutate a prop directly inside a child component?
Explain the flow of data when a user clicks 'Update Name' in a child modal—how does the change reach the main dashboard list?
How would you use `defineProps` and `defineEmits` to build a generic, reusable SearchInput component?

slides.title: Reactive State (ref, reactive)
slides.content: Reactivity is the 'magic' that makes modern web apps feel fast and responsive. In Vue 3, we define our reactive state using `ref` for primitive values and `reactive` for objects. When a reactive variable is changed, Vue's internal engine detects the change via a Proxy and automatically re-renders only the parts of the DOM that are dependent on that data. This 'Fine-Grained Reactivity' is remarkably efficient, as it avoids the overhead of updating the entire page. For example, in our `SiteExplorer`, the list of sites is stored in a `ref`. When a new site is added, Vue detects the change in the array and adds a single new card to the UI in milliseconds. Understanding the difference between `ref` (which requires `.value` in JS) and `reactive` (which works like a standard object) is critical for writing clean, bug-free Vue code. This reactivity system is what allows the FRAISE dashboard to provide real-time updates as AI agents process media tasks or benchmark results stream in.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fffbeb'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='50' fill='#d97706' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#d97706' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M160 100 Q200 60 240 100 T320 100' stroke='#d97706' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='6' fill='#d97706'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#92400e' font-weight='600'>Dynamic State Synchronization</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is the technical difference between a `ref` and a `reactive` object in Vue 3?
Explain how the `Proxy` object allows Vue to detect changes to data without manual 'setters'.
Why do we use `unref` or `toRefs` when passing reactive state as props to child components?

slides.title: Virtual DOM
slides.content: The Virtual DOM is an ingenious performance optimization used by modern libraries like Vue and React. Interacting with the real browser DOM is electronically expensive and slow. To solve this, Vue maintains a lightweight, JavaScript-based representation of the 'ideal' UI in memory. When the application state changes, Vue creates a new Virtual DOM tree and compares it against the previous one using a sophisticated 'diffing' algorithm. It then calculates the absolute minimum number of changes required to update the actual browser DOM—for example, changing a single line of text in a button instead of re-rendering the entire page. This process results in blazing-fast updates and a significantly smoother user experience, especially in data-heavy views like our benchmarks or task managers. By abstracting away the manual DOM manipulation of the jQuery era, the Virtual DOM allows developers to focus on declarative logic while the framework handles the heavy lifting of rendering performance.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><path d='M100 150 L150 150 M250 150 L300 150' stroke='#166534' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='100' height='60' rx='8' fill='#22c55e' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#22c55e' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M180 90 L220 90 M200 70 L200 110' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='2'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#14532d' font-weight='600'>Memory-Mapped UI Registry</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain why directly calculating DOM changes is more efficient than a full page re-render from the server.
How does the 'Diffing' algorithm decide which parts of the Virtual DOM have changed and need updating?
Discuss the trade-offs: when could a Virtual DOM approach actually be slower than simple, direct DOM manipulation?

slides.title: Single Page Application (SPA)
slides.content: A Single Page Application (SPA) is a web application that interacts with the user by dynamically rewriting the current page rather than loading entire new pages from the server. In our FRAISE dashboard, the initial load fetches the HTML, CSS, and Vue JavaScript needed to run the app. Subsequent navigation—like switching from the 'Dashboard' to the 'AI Media Tasks'—happens instantly by swapping out components and updating the URL via the browser's History API. No new HTML files are requested; only raw JSON data is exchanged via API calls. This results in a seamless, 'app-like' experience with no screen flickering or white-flashes between transitions. While traditional Multi-Page Applications (MPAs) like our legacy sites in `repo-php/content/` are great for SEO and simple content, the SPA architecture is the only choice for building the complex, high-interactivity management tools required by modern AI engineering workflows.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf4ff'/><rect x='80' y='40' width='240' height='120' rx='8' fill='#a21caf' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M80 70 H320' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='100' cy='55' r='4' fill='#a21caf'/><circle cx='115' cy='55' r='4' fill='#a21caf'/><circle cx='130' cy='55' r='4' fill='#a21caf'/><rect x='100' y='85' width='200' height='50' rx='4' fill='#a21caf' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#a21caf' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#701a75' font-weight='600'>Dynamic Fluid Transitions</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What are the primary SEO challenges of an SPA and how can SSR (Server-Side Rendering) help solve them?
Explain how the browser's 'PushState' API allows an SPA to change the URL without refreshing the page.
Compare the 'First Contentful Paint' (FCP) of a large SPA against a traditional PHP-rendered MPA.

slides.title: Gemini API
slides.content: The Gemini API is the sophisticated cognitive engine that powers the 'intelligence' within the FRAISE platform. Developed by Google DeepMind, Gemini models (Flash and Pro) are multimodal, meaning they can reason across text, images, and code natively within a single context window. In our architecture, we utilize the `@google/genai` TypeScript SDK to securely communicate with these models from our server-side routes. This allows us to offer features that would previously be impossible: automated SEO metadata generation, intelligent code refactoring suggestions, and AI-driven media asset creation. By leveraging Gemini 1.5's massive 1-million-plus token context window, our agents can 'read' entire repositories at once to understand the complex dependencies of a multi-tenant CMS. This API isn't just a chatbot; it's an engineering primitive that turns our static management dashboard into a proactive, intelligent site-building partner.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><ellipse cx='200' cy='100' rx='90' ry='60' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5'/><path d='M160 100 Q200 40 240 100 T320 100' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='30' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.1'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#1e40af' font-weight='600'>Multimodal Intelligence Core</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What does it mean for an LLM to be 'Multimodal' and how does this benefit a CMS with image and text content?
Explain the benefit of a Large Context Window (1M+ tokens) when performing repository-wide refactoring with an AI agent.
Discuss the security implications of passing user-provided site content to the Gemini API for analysis.

slides.title: AI Agent
slides.content: An AI Agent is a system that goes beyond simple text generation by manifesting 'Agency'—the ability to interact with and change its environment to achieve a goal. Unlike a standard chatbot that just answers questions, an Agent possesses 'Tools.' In our system, these tools include the ability to read and write files, execute shell commands, and interact with our PHP and SQL backends. The 'AI Coder Agent' and 'Media Generator Agent' documented in `docs/ai-agents/` utilize Gemini as their reasoning core, processing instructions and outputting structured JSON that our systems then execute. This loop—Observe, Reason, Act, Evaluate—is what allows an agent to fix a broken layout or migrate a site from site1.com to site2.com autonomously. Mastering agents represents the transition from 'using AI for help' to 'incorporating AI as a teammate' within the software development lifecycle.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fefce8'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='100' height='80' rx='10' fill='#eab308' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M170 90 L190 110 L230 80' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='3' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M100 100 H150 M250 100 H300' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='175' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#854d0e' font-weight='600'>Autonomous Goal-Directed System</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Define 'Agency' in the context of an LLM-powered system. What is the difference between an Agent and a Workflow?
How can the use of 'Tools' (Function Calling) introduce new security vectors in an AI-integrated application?
Design a high-level flowchart for an agent that is tasked with 'Converting this PHP template to a React component'.

slides.title: Prompt Engineering
slides.content: Prompt Engineering is the critical art of communicating with Large Language Models to ensure high-fidelity, predictable outputs. It is far more than just 'asking a question'; it is about defining the persona, context, constraints, and output schema for the AI. In a professional application like ours, we use 'System Prompts' to force the AI to return strictly formatted JSON, preventing the 'chatty' conversational filler that would break a real-world integration. We utilize various techniques: 'Few-Shot Prompting' (providing examples), 'Chain-of-Thought' (asking the AI to reason step-by-step), and 'Negative Constraints' (telling the AI what NOT to do). Effective prompt engineering is what ensures the 'Media Generator' creates valid SVG paths instead of just describing an image. As software architectures become more agentic, the ability to write robust, version-controlled prompts is becoming as essential as writing robust, version-controlled code.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f5f3ff'/><path d='M100 60 H300 V140 H100 Z' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 90 L280 90 M120 110 L220 110' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M250 110 L280 110' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='3'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#5b21b6' font-weight='600'>Structured Cognitive Orchestration</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the 'Few-Shot Prompting' technique and how it improves the accuracy of structured data generation.
Why is 'Chain-of-Thought' prompting critical for complex logical tasks like software refactoring or mathematical reasoning?
Compare 'System Prompts' vs 'User Prompts' in the context of a secure, production-level AI application.

slides.title: Function Calling (AI)
slides.content: Function Calling is the 'Action Bridge' in modern AI engineering. It allows a Large Language Model to move beyond static text generation and actually execute code or interact with external systems. When the model detects that it needs to perform an operation—such as fetching a site's analytics or calculating a benchmark score—it 'calls' a predefined tool by outputting a structured JSON object. Our system then detects this request, executes the corresponding TypeScript or PHP function, and feeds the result back to the AI. This creates a two-way dialogue between 'AI Reasoning' and 'Code Execution.' In the FRAISE platform, function calling is managed by the `AITaskManager`, which acts as a safety gate to ensure that AI-requested actions are validated and authorized before they affect the production cluster. This capability is what transforms our CMS into an autonomous, action-oriented engineering partner.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><path d='M100 80 L180 80 M220 80 L300 80' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><circle cx='200' cy='80' r='20' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M190 80 L210 80' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='120' width='100' height='40' rx='4' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#115e59' font-weight='600'>Structured Action Bridge</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain how Function Calling prevents an LLM from 'hallucinating' data by grounding it in real-world API responses.
Design a JSON schema for a 'generateSite' tool that an AI agent could call to build a new template.
How do we maintain security when allowing an AI model to call functions that have write access to our database?

slides.title: LLM (Large Language Model)
slides.content: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a sophisticated neural network trained on a massive corpus of human knowledge. Models like Google's Gemini utilize the Transformer architecture to predict the next most likely 'token' in a sequence with uncanny accuracy. This statistical prowess allows them to understand not just natural language, but the rigorous syntax of programming languages like Rust, PHP, and Go. Within the FRAISE ecosystem, Gemini acts as the 'Universal Translator' and 'Cognitive Backbone,' assisting developers in complex tasks like legacy code refactoring, multi-tenant site generation, and semantic data analysis. These models are 'pre-trained' on trillions of parameters, giving them a broad understanding of the world, which we then 'fine-tune' using specific system prompts to perform specialized roles as 'AI Coder Agents' or 'DevOps Orchestrators.'
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#eff6ff'/><rect x='100' y='50' width='200' height='100' rx='20' fill='#2563eb' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='150' cy='100' r='10' fill='#3b82f6'/><circle cx='200' cy='100' r='10' fill='#3b82f6'/><circle cx='250' cy='100' r='10' fill='#3b82f6'/><path d='M150 100 H250' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#1e40af' font-weight='600'>Vast Cognitive Neural Mesh</text></svg>
studentPrompts: What is a 'Token' in the context of an LLM and how does it differ from a simple 'Word'?
Explain the 'Transformer' architecture and why it revolutionized the field of Natural Language Processing.
How can 'Temperature' settings affect the creativity vs predictability of an LLM's output during code generation?

slides.title: Coolify
slides.content: Coolify is the open-source power-house that manages our production environment. Think of it as a self-hostable alternative to platforms like Heroku or Vercel, providing an 'easy button' for complex Docker deployments. Coolify takes our multi-tenant source code, builds the necessary images, orchestrates the private networking between containers, and manages our SSL certificates using Traefik as the reverse proxy. It provides a beautiful dashboard for monitoring logs, managing environment variables, and triggering zero-downtime deployments. By using Coolify, we retain total control over our data—we can host our CMS on any cloud provider (AWS, GCP, DigitalOcean) while enjoying the convenience of a modern, automated deployment pipeline. It is the 'Ops' in our DevOps story, ensuring that our polyglot application is stable, secure, and ready to scale at a moment's notice.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fefce8'/><path d='M100 150 L200 50 L300 150 Z' fill='#eab308' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M180 120 L200 100 L220 120' stroke='#eab308' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><rect x='120' y='150' width='160' height='20' rx='4' fill='#eab308' fill-opacity='0.2'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#854d0e' font-weight='600'>Self-Hosted Deployment Control</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why would a company choose a self-hosted solution like Coolify instead of a fully managed service like Vercel?
Explain the role of Traefik in a Coolify-managed environment for handling SSL and routing.
How does Coolify achieve 'Zero-Downtime' deployments for a multi-tenant Docker application?

slides.title: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Deployment)
slides.content: CI/CD is the operational philosophy of 'moving fast without breaking things.' Continuous Integration (CI) refers to the practice of automatically running tests and linters every time a developer commits code to the repository. This ensures that new features don't introduce regressions or security vulnerabilities. Continuous Deployment (CD) is the next step, where successfully tested code is automatically deployed to production containers. In our stack, this pipeline is critical for maintaining consistency across our PHP, Node, and Python services. A robust CI/CD workflow eliminates manual deployment errors and significantly reduces the 'Time to Market' for new features. It turns the terrifying event of 'Deploying to Production' into a routine, automated, and boring non-event, allowing our team to focus on building value rather than managing servers.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdf4'/><path d='M100 100 Q200 40 300 100 T100 100' stroke='#166534' stroke-width='2' fill='none' stroke-dasharray='4'/><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='5' fill='#166534'/><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='5' fill='#166534'/><text x='200' y='170' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#14532d' font-weight='600'>Automated Infinite Workflow</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Compare 'Continuous Delivery' vs 'Continuous Deployment'—which one is more suitable for a high-risk banking application?
How can a Linter within a CI pipeline prevent 'Technical Debt' from accumulating over time?
Explain how a 'Staging Environment' fits into an automated CD pipeline for a multi-tenant CMS.

slides.title: Webhooks
slides.content: Webhooks are the 'Event Notifications' of the internet. They allow one application to send real-time data to another application the moment an event occurs. Unlike 'Polling'—where your application repeatedly asks 'Do you have new data?'—a webhook is a 'Push' mechanism. For example, when a user submits a contact form on `site2.com`, that site's frontend triggers a webhook to our central FRAISE dashboard. Our API receives the payload, processes the submission, and triggers an email notification immediately. Webhooks are essential for scaling microservices as they minimize unnecessary network traffic and ensure that data is synchronized across disparate systems in milliseconds. Mastering webhooks allows you to build deeply integrated ecosystems where external tools like Stripe, GitHub, or Coolify can communicate directly with your application core.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fff7ed'/><path d='M100 100 L300 100' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><path d='M280 90 L300 100 L280 110' fill='none' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='80' width='40' height='40' rx='4' fill='#ea580c' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='300' y='80' width='40' height='40' rx='4' fill='#ea580c' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#ea580c' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#9a3412' font-weight='600'>Event-Driven Data Injection</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the performance benefits of Webhooks over AJAX Polling in a real-time dashboard environment.
How do you verify the 'Authenticity' of an incoming webhook to prevent attackers from injecting fake data?
Describe a scenario where a failure in a webhook delivery could cause data inconsistency and how to mitigate it (e.g., retries).

slides.title: SEO (Search Engine Optimization)
slides.content: Search Engine Optimization (SEO) is the science of ensuring your content is discoverable by the algorithms that navigate the web. In the FRAISE ecosystem, we treat SEO as a first-class engineering concern, not just an afterthought. Our `seo-optimizer` plugin automates the generation of technical metadata, sitemaps, and 'Open Graph' tags that ensure your site looks professional when shared on social media. Furthermore, we leverage the Gemini API to analyze page content and suggest high-impact keywords, title improvements, and semantic structure adjustments. By utilizing semantic HTML tags like `<article>`, `<section>`, and proper `<h1>-<h6>` hierarchies, we provide search engines with a clear roadmap of our content. A well-optimized site doesn't just rank higher; it provides a better user experience by being more accessible and informative to both humans and machines.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0f9ff'/><path d='M100 140 L180 80 L240 110 L320 40' stroke='#38bdf8' stroke-width='4' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='320' cy='40' r='6' fill='#0ea5e9'/><path d='M100 160 H320' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#075985' font-weight='600'>Algorithmic Visibility Growth</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How does semantic HTML (e.g., using `<main>` and `<nav>`) improve a site's SEO compared to generic `<div>` layouts?
Explain the role of 'Canonical URLs' in preventing search engines from penalizing duplicate content in a multi-tenant CMS.
Design an AI prompt that could analyze a blog post and generate a JSON object containing optimized `title`, `description`, and `keywords` tags.

slides.title: Analytics Plugins
slides.content: Analytics are the 'eyes' of a digital product, allowing you to observe how users navigate and interact with your creation. In our multi-tenant CMS, the `analytics` plugin provides a modular way to inject tracking scripts—like Google Analytics, Plausible, or Matomo—into every site instance without modifying the core templates. This 'Inversion of Control' ensures that each tenant (e.g., `babiblog.fr`) can have its own unique tracking ID and data privacy settings. Beyond simple pageview counts, modern analytics can track 'Events' like button clicks, form submissions, and media plays, providing a deep map of the user journey. By analyzing this data, we can make informed, evidence-based decisions about UI improvements, content strategy, and resource allocation, ensuring that the FRAISE platform remains optimized for the real-world behaviors of its users.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fdf2f8'/><rect x='100' y='120' width='30' height='30' rx='4' fill='#db2777' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='80' width='30' height='70' rx='4' fill='#db2777' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='200' y='100' width='30' height='50' rx='4' fill='#db2777' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='250' y='60' width='30' height='90' rx='4' fill='#db2777' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#db2777' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#831843' font-weight='600'>Data-Driven Behavior Maps</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Discuss the privacy implications of third-party tracking scripts and how 'Privacy-First' analytics like Plausible differ from Google Analytics.
How would you modify the Analytics plugin to support custom event tracking for 'Media Play' events in repo-php?
Why is it important to filter out developer 'Localhost' traffic from production analytics data?

slides.title: Form Management
slides.content: Forms are the primary interaction point between a website and its visitors, taking everything from simple contact requests to complex registration data. In our CMS, form management is handled by a sophisticated plugin in `repo-php/plugins/forms/`. It includes a 'Form Builder' that allows site owners to define fields, validation rules, and success messages as a JSON schema. When a form is submitted, the system performs server-side validation to ensure data integrity and security, protecting against malicious inputs via 'Honeypots' and 'Sanitization.' Submissions are then persisted in the database and can be exported or viewed within the Vue dashboard via a secure API bridge. This end-to-end management ensures that user data is never lost and that communication remains a reliable, automated process for every site in the multi-tenant cluster.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0fdfa'/><path d='M120 60 H280 V160 H120 Z' fill='#0d9488' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M140 90 H260 M140 110 H260 M140 130 H200' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M250 140 L260 150 L275 130' stroke='#0d9488' stroke-width='2' fill='none' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#115e59' font-weight='600'>Relational Input Orchestration</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is Client-Side validation insufficient for a secure web application? Why MUST we also validate on the server?
Explain the 'Honeypot' technique for preventing automated bot submissions without using a complex CAPTCHA.
How can we use AI to analyze form submissions and automatically categorize them as 'Leads', 'Support', or 'Spam'?

slides.title: Template Hierarchy
slides.content: The Template Hierarchy is the logical brain that decides which PHP file should be used to render a specific page request. Heavily inspired by WordPress, our hierarchy system in `repo-php/class/Hierarchy.php` allows for extreme design flexibility without complex routing rules. When a request for a 'Blog Post' comes in, the hierarchy looks for `single.php`. If that doesn't exist, it falls back to `index.php`. For a 'Custom Landing Page,' it might look for `template-landing.php`. This 'Fallback Chain' allows us to build powerful, customizable themes where a site owner can override specific layouts just by adding a new file to their content folder. Understanding this hierarchy is the key to mastering the custom theme development within the FRAISE multi-tenant CMS, as it determines how data from the Models is ultimately transformed into the beautiful, interactive Views seen by the user.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f5f3ff'/><rect x='150' y='40' width='100' height='30' rx='4' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='150' y='90' width='100' height='30' rx='4' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.1' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='150' y='140' width='100' height='30' rx='4' fill='#7c3aed' fill-opacity='0.05' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='2'/><path d='M200 70 V90 M200 120 V140' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='200' y='185' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#5b21b6' font-weight='600'>Thematic Rendering Fallback</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Explain the concept of 'Template Fallback' and how it improves developer efficiency in a theme-based CMS.
How can the Template Hierarchy be used to create uniquely styled search results pages without modifying the core PHP router?
Design a custom hierarchy rule for a site that needs a special layout for all articles in the 'Technology' category.

slides.title: Benchmarking
slides.content: Benchmarking is the objective measure of an application's performance and efficiency. In the FRAISE platform, we use our `SiteBenchmarker.vue` tool to analyze critical metrics like 'Time to First Byte' (TTFB), 'First Contentful Paint' (FCP), and total payload size. Because we operate a polyglot stack, benchmarking allows us to provide scientific evidence for which language excels in specific scenarios—for example, comparing our Rust-based media processing against traditional PHP implementations. These benchmarks help us identify bottlenecks, optimize database queries, and ensure that our AI-generated features don't introduce unacceptable latency for the end user. In a professional engineering environment, 'guessing' about performance is unacceptable; benchmarking provides the raw data needed to make informed architectural decisions that ensure our platform remains fast, scalable, and cost-effective as it grows.

studentPrompts: What is 'Time to First Byte' (TTFB) and why is it a critical metric for a PHP-rendered CMS?
Explain how the 'First Contentful Paint' metric directly impacts user retention and SEO ranking.
How would you use Apache Benchmark (ab) or k6 to stress-test the FRAISE API under high concurrent load?
